recipes.name: ANZAC Biscuits
steps: Preheat oven to 160°C. Line baking sheets with parchment paper.
In a bowl, mix oats, coconut, flour, and sugar.
In a saucepan, melt butter and golden syrup together.
Dissolve baking soda in boiling water and add to the butter mixture.
Pour the wet mixture into the dry ingredients and mix well.
Roll tablespoon-sized portions into balls and place on baking sheets.
Cool on the baking sheet for 5 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack.
making_tips: ANZAC biscuits were originally sent to soldiers in World War I.
They contain no eggs so they kept well during transport.
The biscuits should be chewy in the center and crisp at the edges.
Golden syrup is essential for the authentic flavor.
Store in an airtight container.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial ANZAC biscuits are often too sweet. Homemade have the perfect texture and oat flavor.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Oats provide fiber and B vitamins. Coconut offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Lamingtons
steps: Preheat oven to 180°C. Grease a 20x30cm baking pan.
Make Cake: Beat butter and sugar until fluffy. Add eggs one at a time.
Add vanilla. Fold in flour alternately with milk.
Pour into the pan and bake for 25-30 minutes.
Make Chocolate Coating: Sift icing sugar and cocoa. Add boiling water and butter. Mix until smooth.
Dip each cake square into the chocolate coating.
Place on a wire rack to set.
making_tips: The cake should be slightly dry to absorb the chocolate coating.
Work quickly—the chocolate sets fast.
Coconut should be fine and fresh.
Lamingtons are an Australian icon.
Can be filled with jam or cream.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial lamingtons are often dry. Homemade are fresh and chocolatey.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cocoa provides antioxidants. Coconut offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Pavlova
steps: Preheat oven to 150°C. Line a baking sheet with parchment.
Beat egg whites until stiff peaks form.
Gradually add sugar, beating until thick and glossy.
Fold in vinegar, cornstarch, and vanilla.
Spoon onto the baking sheet into a round mound.
Spread into a circle, creating a slight well in the center.
Turn off oven and leave pavlova inside to cool completely.
Whip cream with powdered sugar to soft peaks.
Top pavlova with cream and fresh fruit.
making_tips: The vinegar and cornstarch give pavlova its marshmallow center.
Don't open the oven door during baking.
The pavlova should be crisp on the outside and soft inside.
The debate between Australia and New Zealand over its origin continues.
Top with passionfruit for the classic presentation.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Store-bought pavlova is often too crisp. Homemade has the perfect marshmallow interior.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Egg whites provide protein. Fresh fruit adds vitamins and antioxidants.

recipes.name: Fairy Bread
steps: Spread butter generously on each slice of bread.
Sprinkle hundreds and thousands over the buttered surface.
Press gently to make the sprinkles stick.
making_tips: Fairy bread is Australia's iconic children's party food.
White bread is traditional—use the freshest bread.
The butter should be soft enough to spread but not melted.
The sprinkles must be round (hundreds and thousands).
Never use any other type of sprinkle—this is law.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This is a homemade classic that can't be bought.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Butter provides vitamin A. The dish is a nostalgic treat.

recipes.name: Flat White
steps: Brew a strong espresso using a machine or stovetop pot.
Heat milk until steaming. Froth until microfoam forms.
Pour steamed milk over the espresso, holding back the foam.
Top with a thin layer of microfoam.
making_tips: The flat white has a thin layer of microfoam—not thick like a latte.
The coffee should be strong and rich.
The milk should be velvety, not foamy.
Originating in Australia and New Zealand.
A true flat white is served in a tulip cup.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Cafe versions often use too much foam. Homemade has the perfect balance.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coffee provides antioxidants. Milk adds calcium.

recipes.name: Lemon Lime Bitters
steps: In a shaker, combine citrus juice and simple syrup.
Pour into glasses filled with ice.
Garnish with lemon and lime slices.
making_tips: Aromatic bitters are essential—this is what makes it a 'bitters' drink.
Lemon lime bitters is Australia's pub favorite.
The non-alcoholic version is just as popular.
Use fresh citrus for the best flavor.
The bitters should be added last.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled versions have preservatives. Homemade is fresh and balanced.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Lemons and limes provide vitamin C.

recipes.name: Afghan Cookies (New Zealand)
steps: Preheat oven to 180°C. Line baking sheets.
Cream butter and sugar until fluffy. Add vanilla.
Sift flour and cocoa. Add to butter mixture.
Roll into balls and place on baking sheets.
Make Icing: Mix icing sugar, cocoa, butter, and milk until smooth.
making_tips: Afghan cookies are a New Zealand icon.
Cornflakes give them their unique texture.
The icing should be thick and glossy.
Each cookie must have a walnut half on top.
They are a Kiwi baking classic.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial versions can't match the homemade chocolate-crunch texture.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Walnuts provide healthy fats and omega-3s. Cocoa offers antioxidants.

recipes.name: Hokey Pokey Ice Cream
steps: Make Hokey Pokey: Heat sugar and golden syrup in a pot until dissolved.
Boil until it turns golden and reaches 150°C.
Remove from heat. Add baking soda and stir vigorously—it will foam.
Pour onto a baking sheet. Cool and break into pieces.
Make Ice Cream: Whip heavy cream to soft peaks.
Fold in condensed milk and vanilla.
Fold in the hokey pokey pieces.
Pour into a container and freeze for at least 4 hours.
Serve scoops of the ice cream.
making_tips: Hokey pokey is the Kiwi name for honeycomb.
The baking soda creates the bubbles.
Work quickly when adding baking soda—it sets fast.
The ice cream should be creamy with crunchy honeycomb.
A New Zealand summer favorite.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial hokey pokey ice cream often lacks crunchy bits. Homemade has generous pieces.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cream provides calcium. The dish is a treat.

recipes.name: Kiwi Limeade
steps: Combine kiwi puree, lime juice, and sugar.
Garnish with kiwi and lime slices.
making_tips: The kiwi should be ripe and sweet.
This is a refreshing Kiwi summer drink.
Adjust sweetness to taste.
The drink should be bright green.
Serve ice-cold.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled limeade has preservatives. Homemade is fresh and vibrant.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Kiwifruit provides vitamin C and fiber. Limes offer additional vitamin C.

recipes.name: Cassava Cake (Fiji)
steps: Preheat oven to 180°C. Grease a baking dish.
Squeeze excess liquid from grated cassava.
In a bowl, combine cassava, coconut milk, sugar, eggs, vanilla, and salt.
Bake for 45-50 minutes until golden and set.
making_tips: Cassava must be squeezed well to remove moisture.
This is a Fijian dessert favorite.
The texture should be soft and pudding-like.
Coconut milk is essential for the island flavor.
Serve with fresh fruit.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This traditional pudding is best made fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cassava provides carbohydrates. Coconut offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Coconut Balls (Fiji)
steps: In a bowl, combine coconut, condensed milk, ginger, and vanilla.
Mix well until it forms a thick paste.
making_tips: The ginger gives Fijian coconut candy its unique warmth.
Chill the mixture before rolling.
These are an island favorite.
They should be sweet and coconutty.
Store in the refrigerator.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial versions have additives. Homemade have real ginger and coconut.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coconut provides healthy fats and fiber. Ginger aids digestion.

recipes.name: Fijian Kava (Non-Alcoholic)
steps: Place kava powder in a cloth bag or strainer.
Knead and squeeze the bag in room temperature water for 15 minutes.
The water will turn cloudy and milky.
Add sugar or honey to sweeten.
making_tips: Kava is a traditional Fijian drink used in ceremony.
The drink has a peppery, earthy taste.
Sweetening makes it more palatable.
It should be consumed immediately.
In Fiji, it's drunk from coconut shell cups.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled kava is less authentic. Freshly prepared is the traditional way.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Kava has relaxing properties. Sweetener adds energy.

recipes.name: Cassava Puddings (Solomon Islands)
steps: Mix cassava, coconut milk, sugar, eggs, vanilla, and salt.
Pour into a greased baking dish.
making_tips: The pudding should be soft and slightly chewy.
Coconut milk is the key island ingredient.
This is a Solomon Islands staple.
Can be served warm or chilled.
Often eaten as a snack.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Freshly made island pudding is far superior.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cassava provides carbohydrates. Coconut offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Solomon Tea (Ginger Lemon Tea)
steps: Boil water with grated ginger for 10 minutes.
Add tea bags and steep for 5 minutes.
making_tips: Solomon tea is a comforting island drink.
The ginger gives it a warming kick.
Fresh lemons are essential.
Honey can be used instead of sugar.
This is served year-round.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled versions can't match the fresh ginger and lemon flavor.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Ginger aids digestion. Lemons provide vitamin C.

recipes.name: Pudding de Taro (Vanuatu)
steps: Squeeze excess liquid from grated taro.
Mix taro, coconut milk, sugar, eggs, vanilla, and salt.
Pour into a greased baking dish.
making_tips: Taro pudding is a Vanuatu staple.
The texture should be soft and coconutty.
This is a traditional island dessert.
Can be served with fresh fruit.
A common snack throughout the Pacific.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Island-style pudding is best when freshly baked.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Taro provides fiber and potassium. Coconut milk offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Lime Squash (Vanuatu)
steps: In a pitcher, combine lime juice, sugar, and water.
To serve, fill glasses with ice.
making_tips: Lime squash is a refreshing Vanuatu drink.
Adjust sweetness to taste.
The soda water adds fizz.
Serve ice-cold.
A great refresher in tropical heat.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Freshly made lime squash is far superior to bottled versions.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Limes provide vitamin C.

recipes.name: Panikeke (Samoan Doughnuts)
steps: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt.
Add warm water, egg, and coconut milk if using.
Punch down dough. Divide into 15 pieces.
Shape each into a ball and flatten slightly.
Fry until golden and puffed, turning once.
making_tips: Panikeke is Samoa's favorite sweet bread.
The dough should be soft and light.
Coconut milk adds island flavor.
They are often eaten with tea.
Best served fresh.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh panikeke is a Pacific island treat.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Egg provides protein. Coconut milk offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Koko Samoan (Samoan Cocoa)

Main: 4 tbsp Samoan cocoa powder (or dark cocoa)

steps: In a pot, combine cocoa powder and water.
Heat while whisking until it comes to a boil.
Add coconut milk, sugar, and vanilla.
making_tips: Koko Samoa is a thick, rich drinking chocolate.
Coconut milk is essential for the Samoan version.
It's traditionally served at breakfast.
The cocoa is not overly sweet.
A comforting island beverage.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled versions can't match the rich, coconutty flavor.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cocoa provides antioxidants. Coconut milk offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Fa'ausi (Samoan Coconut Pudding)
steps: In a pot, combine coconut cream, brown sugar, and salt.
Dissolve cornstarch in a little water.
Add to the pot and whisk.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly.
making_tips: Fa'ausi is a rich Samoan coconut pudding.
It should be thick and creamy.
The caramel flavor from brown sugar is key.
Serve cold for the best texture.
A traditional Samoan dessert.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This is a traditional island dessert made fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coconut cream provides healthy fats. Brown sugar offers trace minerals.

recipes.name: Otai (Tongan Watermelon Drink)
steps: Mash or blend watermelon until it's chunky but not pureed.
making_tips: Otai is Tonga's national drink.
The watermelon should be very ripe.
The texture should be chunky, not smooth.
It's a sweet, refreshing tropical drink.
Serve ice-cold.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This is a fresh drink that can't be bottled.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Watermelon provides hydration and vitamin C. Coconut milk offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Manioke (Tongan Cassava Pudding)
steps: Mix cassava, coconut milk, sugar, eggs, vanilla, and salt.
Pour into a greased baking dish.
making_tips: Manioke is a Tongan cassava pudding.
It's a staple dessert across the Pacific.
The coconut milk is essential.
Serve with fresh fruit.
A comforting island dish.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Freshly made manoke is the best.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cassava provides carbohydrates. Coconut offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Bwabwai (Kiribati Coconut Dessert)
steps: Crack coconuts and scrape the meat.
Cook over low heat until thick.
making_tips: Bwabwai is a traditional Kiribati coconut dessert.
The fermentation gives it a unique flavor.
The fresh method is quicker and sweeter.
This is a labor of love in Kiribati.
Serve cold.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This is a traditional island dessert made fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coconut provides healthy fats and fiber.

recipes.name: Pandanus Drink (Kiribati)
steps: If using leaves, bruise and boil in water for 20 minutes.
making_tips: Pandanus gives a distinctive island flavor.
The drink is sweet and fragrant.
Pandanus is a staple flavor across the Pacific.
Serve ice-cold.
A refreshing island drink.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh pandanus drink is a unique island experience.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Pandanus provides trace nutrients. Lime offers vitamin C.

recipes.name: Rukau (Cook Islands Taro Leaves)
steps: Cover and cook over low heat for 30-40 minutes.
The leaves will wilt and cook down.
making_tips: Rukau is a traditional Cook Islands dish.
Taro leaves are the main ingredient.
This sweet version is a dessert.
Coconut milk is essential.
Serve with breadfruit or rice.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This traditional dish is best homemade.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Taro leaves provide iron and calcium. Coconut offers healthy fats.

recipes.name: Poke (Cook Islands Coconut Pudding)
steps: Dissolve cornstarch in a little water.
In a pot, combine coconut cream, sugar, and salt.
Add cornstarch and cook over medium heat.
Stir constantly until thick and glossy.
making_tips: Poke is a Cook Islands coconut pudding.
It's similar to other Pacific puddings.
Fresh fruit is essential on top.
The texture should be silky and set.
A simple and satisfying dessert.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Freshly made poke with tropical fruit is unbeatable.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coconut cream provides healthy fats. Fresh fruit adds vitamins.

recipes.name: Tropical Fruit Punch (Cook Islands)
steps: Blend pineapple with a little water and strain.
making_tips: This is the Cook Islands tropical punch.
Passionfruit gives it signature island flavor.
The pineapple should be sweet and ripe.
Serve ice-cold.
A refreshing taste of the Pacific.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Homemade punch uses real tropical fruit, not syrup.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Passionfruit and pineapple provide vitamin C. Oranges add additional vitamin C.
